audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel 2 draft aligned to Hannah's song, priestly food extraction, reported sexual abuse, Samuel's growth, and oracle against Eli's house; later source/witness audit required.
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Hebrew-first naming, restored/mystical separation, narrative ambiguity, institutional power, vulnerable persons, violence, and misuse risks.

verses.restored: Hannah prayed and said: My heart exults in YHWH; my horn is raised in YHWH. My mouth opens wide over my enemies, because I rejoice in your deliverance.
There is none holy like YHWH, for there is none besides you, and there is no rock like our God.
Do not multiply speaking so proudly, proudly; let arrogance not come out from your mouth. For a God of knowledge is YHWH, and by him deeds are weighed.
Bows of mighty ones are shattered, while those who stumble gird on strength.
Those who were full hire themselves out for bread, while hungry ones cease from hunger. Even the barren one bears seven, while she with many children languishes.
YHWH puts to death and makes alive; he brings down to Sheol and raises up.
YHWH makes poor and makes rich; he brings low, also lifts up.
He raises the poor from dust; from ash heap he lifts the needy, to seat them with nobles and make them inherit a throne of honor. For pillars of earth belong to YHWH, and he sets the world upon them.
Feet of his faithful ones he guards, but wicked ones are silenced in darkness, for not by strength does a human prevail.
Those contending with YHWH are shattered; against them he thunders in heavens. YHWH judges ends of earth; he gives strength to his king and raises horn of his anointed.
Elkanah went to Ramah, to his house. The boy was ministering to YHWH before Eli the priest.
Now sons of Eli were sons of worthlessness; they did not know YHWH.
The custom of priests with the people was this: whenever any human sacrificed a sacrifice, servant of priest came while meat was boiling, with a three-pronged fork in his hand.
He thrust it into basin, kettle, cauldron, or pot. Everything the fork brought up, priest took for himself. Thus they did in Shiloh to all Israel who came there.
Even before they made fat smoke, servant of priest came and said to person sacrificing, Give meat to roast for priest, for he will not take boiled meat from you, but raw.
If the person said to him, Let them first make fat smoke, then take for yourself as your soul desires, he would say, No, but you shall give now; and if not, I will take by force.
Sin of young men was very great before face of YHWH, because the men treated offering of YHWH with contempt.
Samuel was ministering before face of YHWH, a boy girded with a linen ephod.
His mother would make for him a small robe and bring it up to him from days to days, when she came up with her husband to sacrifice sacrifice of days.
Eli blessed Elkanah and his wife and said, May YHWH establish offspring for you from this woman in place of petition/lent one who was asked for YHWH. Then they went to his place.
YHWH attended Hannah, and she conceived and bore three sons and two daughters. The boy Samuel grew with YHWH.
Eli was very old, and he heard all that his sons were doing to all Israel, and how they lay with women who served at entrance of tent of meeting.
He said to them, Why do you do things like these? For I hear of your evil deeds from all this people.
No, my sons, for report that I hear is not good, causing people of YHWH to transgress.

If a human sins against a human, God may mediate for him; but if a human sins against YHWH, who can intercede for him? But they did not listen to voice of their father, for YHWH desired to put them to death.
The boy Samuel continued growing in stature and in goodness with YHWH and also with humans.
A man of God came to Eli and said to him, Thus says YHWH: Did I not reveal myself to house of your father when they were in Egypt under house of Pharaoh?

I chose him from all tribes of Israel for myself as priest, to go up upon my altar, to make incense smoke, to bear ephod before me. I gave to house of your father all fire offerings of children of Israel.
Why do you kick at my sacrifice and at my offering that I commanded in dwelling, and honor your sons above me, making yourselves fat from first of every offering of Israel my people?

Therefore declaration of YHWH, God of Israel: I indeed said your house and house of your father would walk before me perpetually. But now, declaration of YHWH, far be it from me. For those honoring me I honor, and those despising me will be lightly regarded.
Look, days are coming when I will cut off your arm and arm of house of your father, so that there will not be an elder in your house.
You will look upon distress of dwelling amid all good done to Israel, and there will not be an elder in your house all days.
Yet a man of yours I will not cut off from my altar, to consume your eyes and make your soul grieve; and all increase of your house will die as humans.
This is sign for you that will come upon your two sons, upon Hophni and Phinehas: in one day both of them will die.
I will raise for myself a faithful priest; according to what is in my heart and in my soul he will do. I will build for him a faithful house, and he will walk before my anointed all days.
Everyone left in your house will come to bow down to him for a piece of silver and a loaf of bread, and will say, Please attach me to one of priestly offices so I may eat a morsel of bread.

translation_notes: Hannah's song speaks from reversal of humiliation into public theological poetry. It must not be reduced to a fertility testimony or used to promise pregnancy.
Warrior, death, Sheol, poverty, wealth, and judgment imagery are poetic theology, not instructions for harming enemies or blaming impoverished and bereaved people.
Verse 5's 'barren one bears seven' is a reversal image. Fertility is not a measure of holiness, and people with many children are not divinely rejected.
The song's king and anointed references may be literary anticipation or later shaping; they do not sanctify monarchy or erase the anti-monarchical warning of 1 Samuel 8.
Hophni and Phinehas use priestly office to seize food through threat. This is economic and religious abuse, not merely bad manners or ritual technicality.
Verses 13-16 preserve contested priestly portions and fat-burning order; later audit should compare Torah traditions and textual witnesses.
Threatening to take offerings by force shows institutional coercion. Worshipers' compliance cannot be assumed to be consent.

Verse 22 reports that Eli heard his sons lay with women serving at the entrance. The women are not blamed; the passage concerns sexual exploitation or abuse under priestly power, and textual witnesses require audit.
Eli's verbal rebuke is inadequate accountability. Leaders who know of abuse must protect victims, remove access, report wrongdoing where appropriate, and not preserve family power.
The statement that YHWH desired to put the sons to death is difficult theological narration. It cannot excuse institutional inaction, fatalism, execution, or violence against accused persons.
Samuel's growth alongside corruption does not imply that children should remain in unsafe religious environments.
The man of God's oracle condemns Eli for honoring sons above YHWH and benefiting from offerings. Family loyalty does not override survivor safety or justice.
Body-fat language in the oracle critiques enrichment from exploitation; it must not become fat-shaming or a claim that body size reveals morality.
Threats against descendants reflect ancient corporate-household judgment. They may not justify hereditary guilt, collective punishment, or stigmatizing families of offenders.
The faithful-priest promise is not permission for clerical superiority, dynastic replacement violence, or claims that one later religious community invalidates Judaism.
safety_note: Do not romanticize clerical abuse, minimize threats over offerings, blame women serving at the sanctuary, or treat Eli's verbal rebuke as sufficient protection. Do not use Divine-death language for vigilantism or fatalism. Do not fat-shame Eli or apply hereditary punishment to offenders' families. Hannah's song must not become fertility triumphalism or holy-war instruction.
